title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices are Rising as The 8th November CARBâ€™s Workshop is Awaited
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(16thâ€“ 22nd September) â€¢ A total of 247,328 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 17.00% decline week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a rise of 2.60% as 527,654 credits were traded in CA LCFS. â€¢ The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased further this week to $57.83/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, recording a 3.84% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 10.02% in prices. â€¢ The average LCFS spot credit price crossed the $60 mark, rising 6.04% WoW; on 24th Sep, it was $64.67/MT. â€¢ In the RFS market, D3 RIN prices rose by 0.90% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each remained stable WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The LCFS market is showing strong positive trends, as evidenced by an increase in the average LCFS spot credit price and Type 1 transfer prices this week. The market's attention was sparked by CARB's proposal to raise the CI reduction target for 2025 to 9%, up from the initial 5%. It is first time after 26thApril that the spot prices have gone beyond $60/MT. Expected tightening of the market is a major reason for a continual rise in spot prices. Another report by CARB released on 20th September states that emissions from on-road gasoline and diesel vehicles have decreased due to regulations like the Advanced Clean Cars and the LCFS, which mandate the use of cleaner emissions technology and fuels. Similarly, livestock emissions, which account for 70% of agriculture's greenhouse gas emissions, peaked in 2012 but saw reductions again in 2022. This decline is largely attributed to methane digesters funded by California Climate Investments and incentivized by the LCFS. These developments reflect sustenance and positive sentiment, currently surrounding the California LCFS program and highlights how the program is making significant reductions in greenhouse gas emissions across various sectors, even beyond the transportation sector. CARB concluded its public comment analysis on the proposed amendments on 27th August, which cCarbon has thoroughly reviewed. In Oregon, the Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) extended the deadline for public comments on the Climate Protection Program (CPP) from 30th August to 27th September. Similarly, the Washington Department of Ecology has set a deadline of 3rd October for public comments regarding draft changes in the Clean Fuel Standard (CFS) rulemaking. cCarbon will further analyze public sentiments surrounding these proposed rule changes as the deadlines approach.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_588100" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 3.84% WoW rise from $55.69 to $57.83 last week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices rose by 10.02% compared to the previous week. Type 1 trades saw 247,328 credits exchanged, recording a 17.00% WoW fall this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades had risen by 2.60% WoW this week, to 527,654 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_588101" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 6.04% WoW, reaching $60.38/MT. On 24th Sep, the average spot price was $64.67/MT, which significantly rose from $48.17/MT, the lowest point in August. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $65.44/MT as of 24th Sep, up from $57.67/MT as of 10thÂ Sep.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_588102" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 51 trades were reported in the market, with four high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $80.50 for 15,000 credits of Type 2, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $43.00 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 10,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 60,000 credits in Type 1 were undertaken for a price of $57.00, and the smallest volume transfers of 1,000 credits, a Type 1 category, were undertaken for a price of $59.00/MT.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588103" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] Trading activity in August experienced a notable decline, with volumes falling to 1.90 MMT from July's robust 5.21 MMT. While July had witnessed a resurgence in trading to levels reminiscent of April, August saw an uptick in Type 1 prices, which had remained relatively steady during June and July. This upward price trajectory has persisted throughout September. The suggested enhancement of the CI reduction target to 9% for 2025, a significant increase from the initial 5% annual goal, could potentially reinvigorate stakeholder engagement in the market.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588104" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 16th â€“ 22nd Sep, total VPT recorded a 19.09% rise (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 0.90% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices remained stable WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • U.S.-India Strategic Clean Energy Partnership Ministerial Joint Statement. The Strategic Clean Energy Partnership (SCEP) Ministerial was convened by U.S. Energy Secretary Jennifer Granholm and Indian Minister of Petroleum and Natural Gas Hardeep Singh Puri in Washington D.C. The sides reviewed the initiatives undertaken across the technical pillars under the SCEP, including Power & Energy Efficiency, Responsible Oil & Gas, Renewable Energy, Emerging Fuels & Technologies, and Sustainable Growth. The ministers welcomed progress made under the partnership to drive clean energy innovation, strengthen energy security, and accelerate clean energy transitions, including through more focused efforts on clean energy manufacturing and building resilient, responsible, stable, secure and diversified supply chains.
  • Growth Energy Amicus Brief Points Toward EPAâ€™s Lost Opportunity on Biofuels. Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, filed an amicus brief today in a case in the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit challenging the U.S. Environmental Protection Agencyâ€™s (EPA) Multi-Pollutant Emissions Standards for Model Years 2027 and Later Light-Duty and Medium-Duty Vehicles, otherwise known as the â€œtailpipe emissions rule.â€ In the amicus brief, Growth Energy noted that EPAâ€™s rule was a missed opportunity to recognize the positive impact biofuels can have on reducing tailpipe emissions.
  • Simply Blue Group to Develop Major Sustainable Aviation Fuel Hub in Goldboro, Nova Scotia. Simply Blue Group, a leading developer of clean energy projects, has announced the Goldboro site in Nova Scotia, Canada formerly proposed as Goldboro Liquified Natural Gas Project, as the location for their major sustainable aviation fuels hub. This development marks a significant step in the companyâ€™s strategic expansion into North America. The REP in Goldboro will utilise renewable electricity generated from Simply Blue Groupâ€™s solar and wind farms in the Municipality of the District of St. Maryâ€™s. A dedicated transmission line will connect these facilities, ensuring a seamless flow of self-produced power. This renewable energy, along with Nova Scotiaâ€™s sustainable biomass and water resources, will be utilized to produce approximately 150,000 tonnes of SAF per year.
  • Woodland Biofuels' Ambitious Carbon Negative RNG and Ultra-Green Hydrogen Production Facility in Louisiana. In an announcement that could prove to be significant for the United States RNG market, Canada's Woodland Biofuels has committed to make operational an RNG and ultra-green hydrogen plant valued at $ 1.35 billion in the state of Louisiana by the 2028. The project is touted to be the "world's largest carbon negative RNG" facility with carbon sequestering technologies in place. Being the leading RNG producing state, the investment could provide further impetus to the Louisianaâ€™s Climate Action Plan and to proximate clean fuels markets in the country. The project is expected to remove over 800,000 tonnes of CO2 annually, Phases 1 and 2 combined. The inevstment has received significant financial and infrastructure incentives from the state of Louisiana worth close to $250 million.
  • Rio Tinto to Explore Potential of Pongamia Seeds as Feedstock for Renewable Diesel. Global materials and commodities manufacutring giant Rio Tinto has initiated a project to explore the potential of Pongamia seeds as a feedstock for Renewable Diesel. The farms will be set-up in a land area of approximately 3000ha in Queensland, Australia. The effectiveness of the crop will only be assessed 5 years when the crops reach maturity. Pongamia trees, which are native to northern Australia and parts of Asia, possess properties such as high oil and protein content along with carbon capture ability post-seed harvest that make could make them viable sources of biofuel feedstock.
